q: A Saga undoes a partially-completed workflow using…
options: A rollback of the global transaction
why: Saga = sequence of local transactions; on failure it runs compensating transactions to semantically undo prior steps. It trades isolation for availability/eventual consistency.

q: Saga CHOREOGRAPHY (vs orchestration) means…
options: A central coordinator drives every step
Services react to each other's events via pub/sub — no central coordinator
why: Choreography = decentralized, event-driven, each service listens and reacts (no central brain). Orchestration = a central coordinator explicitly drives the steps. (Easy to reverse — anchor it.)

q: Which are Saga isolation/robustness countermeasures a Staff candidate might name? (select all)
why: Semantic lock, pivot transaction, transactional outbox, idempotent compensations, TCC — these recover the isolation Saga gives up. The last option is nonsense.

q: Server-Sent Events (SSE) are best described as…
options: One-way server→client over a single long-lived HTTP connection, with auto-reconnect
why: SSE = one-way server→client over one persistent HTTP connection, with built-in auto-reconnect. Great for notifications/feeds; not for bidirectional chat.

q: You're building a chat app needing low-latency two-way messaging. Best transport?
why: WebSocket = full-duplex over a single persistent connection; right for chat/games. Cost: it's stateful and harder to scale than stateless HTTP.

q: Order these from least to most efficient for server-push: polling, long-polling, SSE. Which is the most efficient of the three?
why: Short polling wastes requests; long polling is better; SSE holds one connection and streams events — most efficient of the three for one-way push.

q: What are the three pillars of observability? (select all)
why: Metrics, logs, traces. Add SLO/SLI/error-budget vocabulary to signal operational maturity.

q: An 'error budget' is…
options: The allowed amount of unreliability = 100% − SLO; once spent, slow feature launches
why: Error budget = 1 − SLO. It quantifies acceptable failure and governs the release/risk trade-off — a strong EM operational signal.

q: Which protect a system under partial failure / overload? (select all)
why: Circuit breakers, backpressure and graceful degradation contain cascading failure. Removing timeouts makes cascades worse.

q: The cleanest mental test for a single point of failure is…
options: 'If this component dies, does the whole system stop?'
why: Walk every component and ask 'if it dies, does the system stop?' Eliminate via redundancy: multiple instances, replicas, multi-AZ, auto-failover, no un-backed shared resource.

q: Which genuinely remove a SPOF? (select all)
options: Multiple stateless instances behind an LB
A single shared NFS mount everything depends on
why: Redundancy at every layer removes SPOFs. A single shared NFS mount everything needs IS a SPOF, not a fix.

q: Rough QPS for 100M daily active users each making ~10 requests/day (uniform)?
why: 1e8 × 10 = 1e9 req/day. 1 day ≈ 86,400s ≈ 1e5. 1e9 / 1e5 ≈ 10,000 → ~11.5k QPS average. Multiply by ~2–3 for peak.

q: Roughly how many seconds are in a day (the number to memorize for QPS math)?
why: 86,400 ≈ 10^5. Memorize it: daily count ÷ 10^5 ≈ average QPS.

q: You store 500M records × 2 KB each. Approximate raw storage?
why: 5e8 × 2e3 bytes = 1e12 bytes ≈ 1 TB. Then add indexes/replication overhead (×3 for RF=3).

q: For high-throughput internal service-to-service calls, what's a common reason to prefer gRPC over REST/JSON?
options: Binary protobuf + HTTP/2 multiplexing → lower latency & payloads, streaming, strong contracts
why: gRPC uses protobuf over HTTP/2: compact binary, multiplexing, bidirectional streaming, codegen contracts. REST/JSON wins for public, human-debuggable, browser-facing APIs.

q: For a list endpoint returning millions of rows, the right pattern is…
options: Force the client to filter client-side
why: Paginate. Cursor/keyset pagination scales better than OFFSET, which degrades on deep pages.

q: HI Redis deep dive: out of the box, what does Redis do when it hits its memory limit?
options: REJECTS new writes — you must configure an eviction policy for a cache
why: Default is noeviction: writes error once memory is full. TTL handles staleness, not memory pressure — for a cache you set allkeys-lru (which Redis approximates by sampling, not exact ordering).

q: HI: in Redis Cluster, what happens if a client asks the wrong node for a key?
options: The node forwards the request for you
The node replies MOVED pointing at the right node; the client refreshes its slot-to-node map
The key is created on that node
why: Each key hashes to one of 16384 slots; clients cache the slot→node map. Ask the wrong node and you get a MOVED reply (nodes gossip the full map but won't forward), so clients aim for the right node first.

q: HI: how do you force two related Redis keys (user:123:posts and user:123:likes) into the same slot?
options: They always land together by default
Hash tags — only the part inside {braces} is hashed, e.g. {user:123}:posts
Use the SAME command for both
why: Only the substring inside {braces} is hashed, so {user:123}:posts and {user:123}:likes share a slot and can be used together in a MULTI transaction. Cluster multi-key ops only work within one slot.

q: HI: why is single-node Redis NOT a system of record, even with a replica?
options: Replication is asynchronous — the primary acks before the replica sees the write, so failover can drop the last acked writes
It has no persistence at all
why: Async replication means a promoted replica may never have seen the last acknowledged writes. Combined with the persistence loss windows (RDB/AOF), acknowledged writes can vanish — so don't make Redis the source of truth.

q: HI: a Redis fixed-window rate limiter does INCR then EXPIRE. What's the subtle bug to avoid?
options: Calling EXPIRE on every request keeps pushing the reset forward — set the TTL only when INCR returns 1 (first request of the window)
You must use a separate key per request
why: If you EXPIRE on every call, steady traffic never gets a fresh window. Set expiry only when INCR returns 1, and run INCR+EXPIRE as one Lua script so a crash between them can't leave a counter that never resets.

q: HI: the correct way to RELEASE a Redis lock (SET key token NX EX 30) is…
options: Check the key still holds YOUR token, then DEL — as one atomic Lua script
why: Your lock may have expired and been re-acquired by someone else; a blind DEL would delete their lock. Check-token-then-DEL must run atomically (Lua) on Redis's single thread.

q: HI: the standard defense against a paused client acting after its Redis lock expired is…
options: A fencing token (monotonically increasing) that the storage layer checks — Redis has nothing built in for this
why: Treat a Redis lock as an efficiency tool that occasionally fails. For correctness, use fencing tokens or a consensus system (ZooKeeper/etcd); often a DB row lock (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) or conditional UPDATE ... WHERE version=X replaces the distributed lock entirely.

q: HI: the old 'Redis Pub/Sub doesn't scale in a cluster' complaint is outdated because…
options: Since Redis 7, sharded Pub/Sub (SPUBLISH/SSUBSCRIBE) routes each channel to the shard that owns its slot, so capacity scales
Pub/Sub uses one connection per channel now
why: Classic cluster Pub/Sub broadcast every message to every node (no added capacity). Sharded Pub/Sub since Redis 7 hashes each channel to a slot. Also: connections are per node, not per channel.

q: HI: remediations for a Redis hot-key problem? (select all)
options: Client-side caching of the hottest keys (short TTL)
Key copies — store under product:123:1..10 so copies hash to different nodes
Read replicas (only if clients are configured for replica reads)
Read replicas to fix a WRITE-hot key
why: Client-side cache, key copies, and read replicas all spread read load. But replicas do nothing for a write-hot key — every write still lands on the one primary that owns the slot.

q: HI: a Redis Stream work queue can process an item twice. Why, and what's the fix?
options: Redis can't tell a slow worker from a dead one, so XCLAIM may re-run a pending entry — make processing idempotent
why: Consumer groups track pending entries by idle time; when a worker seems dead, another XCLAIMs the entry — but a slow worker looks the same as a dead one, so delivery is at-least-once. Make consumers idempotent.

q: In the OSI model as framed by HI, which protocol lives at the Transport Layer (Layer 4)?
why: HI places IP at Layer 3 (Network), TCP/UDP/QUIC at Layer 4 (Transport), and HTTP/WebSockets/DNS/WebRTC at Layer 7 (Application).

q: HI Networking deep dive: a New York to London round trip (~5,600 km) has a theoretical minimum latency of roughly…
why: Light in fiber travels at about 2/3 c (~200,000 km/s), so the ~5,600 km round trip floors near 56ms before any processing, versus under 1ms to a nearby server.

q: HI: why are L4 load balancers generally preferred for WebSocket connections over L7?
options: L4 can inspect HTTP headers to route by cookie
L4 maintains a persistent TCP connection between client and server, as if they were directly connected
L4 terminates the connection and opens a new one to the backend
L4 is more CPU-intensive and therefore more reliable
why: L4 maintains persistent TCP connections (suits WebSockets). L7 terminates connections and inspects content, the opposite behavior.

q: Per HI, which statements about failures and retries are accurate? (select all)
options: 'Retry with exponential backoff' is the magic phrase interviewers want
Backoff should include jitter so clients don't all retry at once
Idempotent APIs make retries safe, often via an idempotency key
Circuit breakers should retry immediately and indefinitely until recovery
why: HI calls 'retry with exponential backoff' the magic phrase, stresses jitter, and idempotency keys for safe write retries. Circuit breakers do the OPPOSITE of retrying indefinitely — they trip open and fail fast.

q: What does HI say about QUIC for interviews?
options: It fully replaces TCP and should always be used
Treat it as a better version of TCP but without the same broad baseline of adoption
It is a Layer 3 routing protocol
It is only used for peer-to-peer video via STUN and TURN
why: HI: consider QUIC a better TCP but lacking TCP's broad adoption; some performance-minded interviewers like QUIC/HTTP3 knowledge, but most want your time elsewhere.

q: Per HI, which HTTP API style is your go-to default unless you have a specific reason otherwise?
why: HI: default to REST; it covers 90% of use cases. If unsure, say 'I'll use REST APIs' and move on.

q: Per HI, which HTTP methods are idempotent? (select all)
why: HI: GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent. POST/PATCH are not guaranteed idempotent — which matters when networks fail and clients retry (you don't want duplicate bookings).

q: HI's biggest GraphQL gotcha: a client queries 100 events each with its venue. What goes wrong?
options: Over-fetching, returning 100x more data than needed
The N+1 problem: roughly 101 DB queries instead of 2
why: HI calls it the N+1 problem: 1 query for events + N for each venue = 101 instead of 2. Fix with batching/dataloader.

q: HI: path parameter vs query parameter — when to use which?
options: Path for optional filters, query for required identifiers
Path when the value is required to identify the resource; query for optional filters
Always query params, easier to test
why: HI ties it to required vs optional: path/nested resource when required (/events/{id}/tickets), query for optional filters (/tickets?event_id=123&section=VIP).

q: HI's recommendation on API versioning in interviews?
options: URL versioning (/v1/events) — widely understood and easy to explain quickly
Semantic versioning in the request body
why: HI recommends URL versioning as the safer interview choice; their own breakdowns omit it only because most interviewers don't care, not because it's unimportant.

q: HI Data Modeling: default database recommendation in an interview?
options: A graph database like Neo4j for relationship-heavy data
A relational DB, specifically PostgreSQL, unless requirements clearly signal otherwise
A document DB like MongoDB for flexibility
A key-value store like Redis as source of truth
why: HI: resist exotic databases; most of the time the right answer is relational, and it specifically recommends PostgreSQL unless you have strong opinions.

q: HI warns graph databases are a common mistake. What point does it make?
options: Even Facebook models its social graph with MySQL, so SQL is good enough for your interview
Graph DBs can't enforce foreign keys
Graph DBs are slower than key-value stores for traversals
why: HI: even Facebook models its social graph on MySQL, and LinkedIn/Twitter use SQL for core relationship data — graph DBs usually add needless complexity.

q: HI: why is time-range sharding usually an anti-pattern for write-heavy systems?
options: Time ranges can't be partition keys
All current writes hit the same shard (the latest range), creating a hot shard
Foreign keys break across time-based shards
why: HI: time-range sharding sends all current writes to the latest-range shard (hot shard). It works better for archival/analytics where writes spread out.

q: Per HI, the three factors that drive schema design decisions? (select all)
why: HI: everything flows from data volume, access patterns (the most important), and consistency requirements. Backend language is not one of them.

q: HI advice on primary keys when defining entities?
options: Use business data like emails so keys are human-readable
Use system-generated IDs (user_id) rather than business data, since they stay stable when business rules change
Always use composite keys from multiple columns
Avoid primary keys in NoSQL designs
why: HI: use system-generated IDs (user_id, post_id) over business data like email, because system keys stay stable even when business rules change.

q: HI: how is main table data typically stored on disk before any index is added?
options: A heap file — rows in no particular order
A hash table keyed by primary key
An LSM tree sorted by timestamp
why: HI describes the main table as a heap file: a collection of rows in no particular order, like a notebook where you write entries as they come.

q: HI: the classic case where indexes hurt more than help?
options: Frequent writes, infrequent reads — like a logging table
Any table over a million rows
A table queried only by primary key
why: HI cites write-heavy, read-light tables (logging) where index maintenance overhead isn't justified; also tiny tables where a sequential scan beats traversing an index.

q: Per HI, which statements about B-tree indexes are correct? (select all)
options: Nodes can have hundreds of children, unlike a binary tree's two
Each node is sized to fit a single disk page (about 8KB)
Finding id=350 in Postgres might read only 2-3 pages from disk
B-trees handle only equality, not range queries
why: HI: B-tree nodes have hundreds of children, fit a ~8KB page, and a lookup may read only 2-3 pages (root, maybe internal, leaf). B-trees handle BOTH equality and range — last option is false.
